questions.question: In a hemodialysis extracorporeal circuit, what device is most commonly used to propel blood through the dialyzer?
questions.explanation: Blood is moved through the dialyzer by a pump, usually a spring-loaded roller pump that completely occludes a small tubing segment and rolls the occluded segment forward, like milking a straw, giving steady positive displacement. Centrifugal impeller and diaphragm pumps are not the standard HD design, and gravity siphon flow cannot generate or control the 200-600 mL/min flow that dialysis needs. Because the roller is self-occluding it delivers a defined stroke volume each pass, which is why blood flow rate depends on pump rpm and the segment's internal volume.

questions.question: The blood line carrying blood from the vascular access to the dialyzer is traditionally called the arterial line, but a more precise term for it would be the:
questions.explanation: The traditional 'arterial' line is more precisely the inflow line, because often only venous blood is actually accessed, such as when a venous catheter is used, so calling it arterial is really a misnomer. Correspondingly, the 'venous' line is more correctly the outflow line running from the dialyzer back to the patient. Return, outflow, and venous all describe the line going back to the patient, not the one feeding the dialyzer, so they are wrong here. The chapter stresses that traditional names persist even though inflow and outflow would be the correct terms.

questions.question: Which set of components is located in the prepump segment of the inflow blood line?

options.A: Heparin infusion T and venous drip chamber
options.B: Blood leak detector and conductivity meter
options.C: Sampling port, saline infusion line, and prepump pressure monitor

questions.explanation: The prepump segment links the patient's access to the blood pump and contains a sampling port, a saline infusion T line, and, on some lines, a prepump (P1) pressure monitor. The sampling port is where predialysis and postdialysis bloods are drawn; the saline T primes the circuit and rinses back the blood compartment at the end of dialysis. The venous drip chamber, air detector, and venous clamp belong to the outflow line, while the blood leak detector and conductivity meter are part of the dialysis fluid circuit, not the prepump blood line.

questions.question: Why is a broken connection at the sampling port, prepump monitor, or saline T especially hazardous?

options.A: They carry the highest positive pressure and blood sprays out
options.B: They contain the heparin syringe, which can empty rapidly
options.C: They are downstream of the venous air detector
options.D: They sit in the negative-pressure portion of the line, so air can quickly enter

questions.explanation: The sampling port, prepump monitor connection, and saline T all sit in the negative-pressure portion of the blood line, because the pump demands blood at a fast rate against access resistance, pulling the pressure below zero. A broken or incomplete connection there can rapidly draw air into the line, forming microbubbles that get trapped in the hollow fibers, reduce dialysis efficiency, and can even lead to clotting of the circuit. This is not the highest positive-pressure region (that is postpump), and these elements sit upstream of, not downstream of, the venous air detector.

questions.question: The blood pump typically demands blood from the vascular access at what flow rate range?
questions.explanation: The blood pump demands blood from the vascular access at a fairly fast rate of 200-600 mL/min, and this demand, combined with resistance at the arterial needle or catheter opening, is what makes the prepump segment pressure negative, often substantially so. The lower ranges (20-60 mL/min) are far too slow for effective solute clearance, while 800-1200 or 1000-2000 mL/min exceed what a vascular access can safely deliver. How negative the prepump pressure becomes depends on this flow rate plus blood viscosity, lumen size, and any needle obstruction.

questions.question: A typical prepump (P1) pressure monitor might be set to alarm and stop the pump if pressure rises above -50 or falls below which value?
questions.explanation: For safety the P1 limits are set above and below the patient's usual working range; the chapter's example alarms if pressure rises above -50 mm Hg or falls below -200 mm Hg. The high (-50) side may signal a line separation that suddenly lowers inflow resistance, while dropping below -200 (e.g., -250) signals obstruction from a kink or a clot at the needle lumen. The other listed values (-100, -150, -300) are not the example limits given. When a limit is crossed, an audible alarm sounds and the blood pump stops so the problem can be investigated.

questions.question: Which statement about using a pressure alarm to detect an inflow line separation is correct?

options.A: It reliably detects every line separation
options.B: It detects separations only at very high blood flows
options.C: It should never be relied upon, as pressure may remain in range after separation
options.D: It detects separations only when hematocrit is low

questions.explanation: The prepump pressure alarm should never be relied upon to detect a line separation, because pressure may remain in range even after the line separates. If there is a partial blockage after separation, or if an arterial needle pulls out but continued resistance keeps pressure in the set range, the alarm may not sound and the pump will keep pumping air into the circuit. It is therefore not a reliable detector at any flow or hematocrit. This limitation is why exposed access sites, careful taping, and dedicated leak sensors matter for safety.

questions.question: Over time, repeated compression of the roller pump insert can cause the tubing to flatten, which:

options.B: Increases the priming volume of the dialyzer
options.C: Increases the effective blood flow rate
options.D: Reduces stroke volume and lowers the effective blood flow rate

questions.explanation: Blood flow through the roller pump equals rpm times the pump-segment volume, so it depends on full stroke volume being delivered each pass. With time, repeated compression and relaxation of the pump insert flattens the tubing, which reduces the stroke volume and can lower the effective blood flow rate; a similar effect occurs with high negative inflow pressure. It does not change dialysate conductivity, priming volume, or raise flow. More rigid tubings and machine correction factors for pump speed and negative pressure are used to counter this error.

questions.question: What is the pressure reading at the postpump (P2) monitor on the inflow line during dialysis?
questions.explanation: The postpump (P2) segment pressure is always positive (above atmospheric) because the pump is pushing blood against downstream dialyzer and access resistance. P2 can be combined with the venous P3 reading to estimate mean blood-compartment pressure and, with the dialysate compartment pressure, to calculate ultrafiltration. It is never uniformly negative, zero, or equal to drain pressure. A sudden rise at P2 is often a sign of impending clotting of the blood line or dialyzer, so trends there are clinically watched.

questions.question: How is heparin delivered at a constant rate during dialysis?

options.A: By a bolus injected into the venous chamber each hour
options.B: By a syringe clamped in a mechanical device that slowly pushes the plunger
options.C: By mixing it into the dialysate concentrate
options.D: By gravity drip into the prepump segment

questions.explanation: Heparin is delivered at a constant rate by a syringe clamped into a mechanical device that slowly pushes on the plunger, infusing through the postpump inflow T. It is not given as hourly boluses into the venous chamber, mixed into dialysate concentrate, or dripped by gravity into the prepump segment. Constant, controlled delivery keeps anticoagulation steady through the treatment. The postpump location is used because that segment is under positive pressure, allowing reliable infusion into the moving blood stream.

questions.question: The outflow (venous) blood line contains all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: The outflow (venous) line contains a venous drip chamber that traps and lets you remove accumulated air, the venous (P3) pressure monitor, and an air detector. The heparin infusion syringe is the exception: it connects via a T on the postpump inflow line, not the venous line. The drip chamber is where incipient clotting usually first appears, causing pressures at both P3 and P2 to rise. The air detector and clamp on this line are key patient-safety devices preventing air return.

questions.question: Which statement about the venous (P3) pressure alarm and line separation is accurate?

options.A: It cannot be relied upon to detect a venous line separation, and patients have exsanguinated
options.B: It always shuts down the pump when a venous line separates
options.C: It fails only with catheters, never with fistulas
options.D: It detects separation best when operating venous pressure is low

questions.explanation: A venous (P3) pressure alarm CANNOT be relied upon to detect a venous line separation, and patients have exsanguinated from continued pump operation when separation went undetected. Detection often fails with AV fistulas, and a separation from a venous catheter may also fail to alarm, particularly when the operating venous pressure is relatively low; if the venous needle pulls out, outflow pressure may barely change because most outflow resistance is in the needle. So it does not always shut down the pump, and it fails with fistulas as well as catheters, making the other options wrong.

questions.question: For patients at high risk of line separation (e.g., cognitive impairment or agitation), what additional safety device can be used?

options.B: A Redsense sensor that detects blood leakage at the connection site

questions.explanation: For patients at high risk of line separation, such as those with cognitive defects, agitation, or who repeatedly uncover their access, an additional device like the Redsense sensor can be used to detect blood leakage at the site of a potential separation. It supplements, and does not replace, effective taping of needle sites and keeping the access always exposed to caregivers' view. A second roller pump, higher conductivity, or ethylene oxide sterilization have nothing to do with detecting separation. This matters because the pressure alarms alone cannot be trusted to catch a separation.

questions.question: When the venous level/air detector senses a drop in blood level (increase in air) at the drip chamber, what happens?

options.A: The dialysate is diverted to bypass only
options.B: The heparin rate is automatically increased
options.C: An alarm sounds, pump power is cut, and a clamp below the chamber snaps shut
options.D: Conductivity is rechecked and dialysis continues

questions.explanation: When the level/air detector at the top of the drip chamber senses a drop in blood level (an increase in air), an alarm sounds, power to the pump is cut so dialysis stops, and a powerful clamp below the drip chamber snaps shut. That clamp prevents any air/blood mixture in the line from passing back into the patient. The response is not to bypass dialysate, raise the heparin rate, or simply recheck conductivity and continue. This multi-step interlock is one of the most important patient-safety features of the outflow line.

questions.question: Which strategy helps limit the release of microbubbles into the patient's circulation during dialysis?

options.A: Running a low fluid level in the venous chamber
options.B: Increasing the blood flow rate above 600 mL/min
options.D: Maintaining a high fluid level in the venous air chamber and using wet-stored dialyzers

questions.explanation: Despite the air detector, microbubbles can still reach the patient, and two strategies limit their release: maintaining a high fluid level in the venous air chamber and using dialyzers supplied pre-filled with fluid in the hollow fibers (wet-stored), as shown by Forsberg 2013. Running a low chamber level would do the opposite, and increasing blood flow above 600 mL/min or choosing ethylene oxide sterilization does not reduce microbubbles. The point is that the air detector alone cannot stop all microbubbles, so these preventive measures still matter.

questions.question: Which of the following is a distinct component of the dialysis fluid (solution) circuit?

options.A: A proportioning system that mixes concentrates with treated water

questions.explanation: The dialysis fluid circuit has distinct components: a stand-alone water purification system, a proportioning system that mixes concentrates with treated water and delivers dialysate, monitors and alarms, ultrafiltration control, and advanced control options. The proportioning system is therefore the correct answer. The arterial sampling port, the roller pump insert, and the heparin syringe pump all belong to the blood circuit. The two circuits meet only at the dialyzer, separated by the semipermeable membrane.

questions.question: Approximately how much water is a patient exposed to across the dialyzer during each dialysis treatment?
questions.explanation: Patients are exposed to about 120-200 L of water during each dialysis treatment, so any small-molecular-weight substance present in the water can cross the dialyzer membrane and enter the bloodstream. This large exposure is why water purity must be monitored and controlled, and the much smaller (10-20 or 50-80 L) or much larger (300-400 L) figures are incorrect. Because of this risk, AAMI has set minimum purity standards for dialysis water, with purification methods detailed in Chapter 5.

questions.question: Which organization has developed minimum standards for the purity of water used in hemodialysis?

options.C: AAMI (Association for the Advancement of Medical Instrumentation)

questions.explanation: The Association for the Advancement of Medical Instrumentation (AAMI) has developed the minimum standards for the purity of water used in hemodialysis. This matters because patients are exposed to 120-200 L of water per treatment and all small-molecular-weight solutes in that water can cross the dialyzer into the blood. The WHO, CDC, and FDA are not credited in this chapter with these dialysis water standards. The specific purification methods behind meeting AAMI limits are covered in Chapter 5.

questions.question: A central dialysis solution delivery system offers lower initial equipment and labor costs, but its main disadvantage is that it:

options.D: Cannot individualize solution composition and exposes many patients to any single error

questions.explanation: A central delivery system produces all the unit's dialysis solution from one apparatus piped to each machine, offering lower initial equipment and labor costs. Its main disadvantage is that it does not permit varying solution composition for individual patients, and any system error exposes large numbers of patients to complications. It does not require ethylene oxide sterilization, still controls temperature, and is not linked to microbubbles. The alternative individual system lets each machine proportion its own concentrate with purified water, allowing patient-by-patient tailoring.

questions.question: Dialysis solution is usually delivered to the dialyzer at which temperature range?
questions.explanation: Dialysis solution must be delivered to the dialyzer at the correct temperature, usually 35-38 degrees C. City water arrives below room temperature and must be heated to this range. The other ranges are wrong: 20-25 or 30-33 degrees C would be uncomfortably cool and risk hypothermia in an unconscious patient, while 40-45 degrees C reaches the dangerous zone. Solution heated above 42 degrees C can cause blood protein denaturation and hemolysis, which is why a temperature sensor and bypass valve guard this limit.

questions.question: Degassing of the heated dialysis water is accomplished by:

options.B: Exposing the heated water to a negative pressure
options.C: Passing the water through the roller pump
options.D: Cooling the water below room temperature

questions.explanation: Degassing is accomplished by exposing the heated water to a negative pressure. As cold city water is heated, dissolved gases expand and bubble out of solution, and the machine must remove this air before the water is used, since dialysate must be free of excessive dissolved air. Adding a chemical antifoaming agent, passing water through the roller pump (which is in the blood circuit), or cooling the water are not how degassing is done. Removing this air prevents bubbles from entering the dialysate compartment during treatment.

questions.question: The normal conductivity range for dialysis solution is:
questions.explanation: The normal conductivity range for dialysis solution is 12-16 mS/cm. Because the primary solutes in dialysate are electrolytes, their concentration is reflected by electrical conductivity, which is continuously monitored in all proportioning systems. Values of 5-10, 20-25, or 30-40 mS/cm fall outside this normal window. If conductivity falls outside preset limits an alarm sounds and a valve diverts dialysate to the drain, so the system 'goes into bypass' and dialysis stops until the problem, such as an empty concentrate container or wrong concentrate, is fixed.

questions.question: One Siemen (the unit underlying conductivity measurement) is equal to the reciprocal of one:
questions.explanation: One Siemen equals the reciprocal of one ohm, and an alternative term for a Siemen is 'mho.' Conductivity, measured in milliSiemens per centimeter, reflects the electrolyte concentration of the dialysis solution, which is why it is used to verify safe composition. It is not the reciprocal of a volt, ampere, or watt. The normal dialysate conductivity range is 12-16 mS/cm, and readings outside preset limits trigger an alarm and diversion of dialysate to the drain to protect the patient.

questions.question: Exposure of blood to a severely hypoosmolar (overly dilute) dialysis solution can result in:

options.A: Rapid hemolysis with severe hyponatremia and hyperkalemia

questions.explanation: Exposure of blood to a severely hypoosmolar (overly dilute) dialysis solution can cause rapid hemolysis with severe hyponatremia and hyperkalemia, as water shifts into and lyses red cells. This is the opposite of a severely hyperosmolar solution, which causes hypernatremia and other electrolyte disturbances, so option B is wrong. It is not merely metabolic alkalosis and is certainly not without consequence. Because these disturbances stem from a proportioning malfunction, continuous conductivity monitoring exists to catch dilute or concentrated dialysate before it reaches the patient.

questions.question: Use of dialysis solution heated above 42 degrees C is dangerous because it can lead to:
questions.explanation: Use of dialysis solution heated above 42 degrees C is dangerous because it can cause blood protein denaturation and ultimately hemolysis. By contrast, cool dialysate down to 35 degrees C is not itself dangerous unless the patient is unconscious, in which case hypothermia can occur; a conscious patient simply feels cold and shivers. So hypothermia, hyponatremia, and air embolism are not the hazard of excess heat. A temperature sensor diverts the fluid to drain when limits are exceeded, protecting the blood from thermal injury.

questions.question: If either dialysis solution conductivity or temperature is out of limits, the bypass valve:

options.B: Injects saline into the blood line
options.C: Diverts dialysis solution around the dialyzer directly to the drain

questions.explanation: If either dialysis solution conductivity or temperature is out of limits, a bypass valve diverts the dialysis solution around the dialyzer directly to the drain, protecting the patient until the problem is resolved. It does not speed the blood pump, inject saline into the blood line, or shut down water purification. This 'going into bypass' means dialysis effectively pauses while the offending dialysate is discarded rather than delivered. The same protective diversion applies whether the trigger is abnormal conductivity or abnormal temperature.

questions.question: Where is the blood leak detector located in the dialysis machine?

options.D: In the dialysate outflow line after fluid passes through the dialyzer

questions.explanation: The blood leak detector is placed in the dialysate outflow line, the line carrying dialysis fluid after it has passed through the dialyzer, because small leaks through the membrane are invisible to the naked eye. If it senses blood there, the alarm activates and blood flow through the dialyzer is stopped automatically to prevent potentially catastrophic blood loss. It is not in the prepump segment, venous drip chamber, or water purification inlet. Detecting membrane failure early on the dialysate side is the whole purpose of positioning it downstream of the dialyzer.

questions.question: In machines without direct UF-control circuitry, the dialysate outflow pressure monitor is used together with the blood outflow pressure to calculate the:

options.A: Transmembrane pressure (TMP), to estimate UF rate

questions.explanation: In machines without special pumps and circuitry to control UF directly, the dialysate outflow pressure monitor is combined with the blood outflow pressure to calculate the dialyzer transmembrane pressure (TMP) and thereby estimate the UF rate. It does not measure conductivity, blood flow rate, or membrane surface area. This manual TMP-based approach is prone to error, which is why more advanced machines use direct volumetric UF control. The blood-side data used are P3 or the average of P2 and P3 together with the dialysate outflow sensor.

questions.question: The most advanced method of ultrafiltration control in modern dialysis machines is:

options.B: A volumetric method using balancing chambers or double-gear systems

questions.explanation: The most advanced UF control is a volumetric method that tracks dialysis solution inflow and matches it to outflow using balancing chambers or double-gear systems, ensuring the volume delivered to the dialyzer equals that removed. A separate line then draws off fluid through a UF pump set by a microprocessor that tracks desired versus total UF. Manual TMP estimation, gravity-based removal, and conductivity-based estimation are the less precise alternatives. Volumetric control is what allows very water-permeable dialyzers to be used safely.

questions.question: Volumetric UF control allows safe use of very water-permeable dialyzers, defined as those with a KUF greater than:
questions.explanation: Volumetric UF circuitry lets even very water-permeable dialyzers, defined as those with KUF greater than 10 mL/h per mm Hg, be used safely, because the balancing system removes exactly the intended volume regardless of membrane permeability. Values of 2 or 5 mL/h per mm Hg are low, not 'very' permeable, and 50 is not the chapter's stated threshold. With such high-KUF membranes, small TMP-setting errors would otherwise cause large fluid-removal errors, so accurate volumetric control is essential.

questions.question: Machines using the three-stream method with a variable bicarbonate option allow delivery of final bicarbonate concentrations in what range?
questions.explanation: Three-stream machines (acid concentrate, bicarbonate concentrate, and water) with a variable bicarbonate option can alter bicarbonate proportioning to deliver final bicarbonate concentrations of 20-40 mM. This is useful in acidotic patients or in those with frank metabolic alkalemia or at high risk of respiratory alkalosis. Ranges like 5-15, 45-60, or 60-80 mM are outside this window. Note the displayed value, estimated from conductivity, omits extra base from acetate or citrate, which may add up to 8 mM, so total base content matters clinically.

questions.question: In machines with a variable sodium option, the dialysate sodium concentration is usually altered by:

options.A: Changing the proportions of acid concentrate and water
options.B: Changing the bicarbonate concentrate proportion only

questions.explanation: The variable sodium option changes dialysate sodium by altering the proportions of acid concentrate and water, adjustable by simply turning a dial. Because the acid concentrate carries other solutes, changing sodium this way also slightly changes the concentration of all those other solutes. It is not done by changing only the bicarbonate concentrate, adjusting temperature, or raising blood flow. This lets sodium be individualized per patient or profiled during treatment, though profiling carries a risk of sodium gain.

questions.question: A potential adverse consequence of using sodium profiling during dialysis is:

options.B: Sodium gain leading to thirst, hypertension, and increased interdialytic fluid intake

questions.explanation: Sodium profiling can expose patients to a potential sodium gain during treatment, leading to thirst, hypertension, and increased fluid intake between treatments. That is the key adverse consequence, not hemolysis (which comes from a hypoosmolar dialysate malfunction), air embolism, or bicarbonate depletion. The variable sodium option achieves the profile by changing the acid-concentrate-to-water proportions during dialysis. So while profiles may aid intradialytic stability, the trade-off is a possible net sodium load that worsens interdialytic fluid gain.

questions.question: The clinical rationale offered for programmable ultrafiltration (front-loading UF) is that patients:

options.A: Clear urea faster later in the session
options.B: Require constant UF to avoid hemolysis
options.C: Can tolerate higher UF rates earlier rather than later in a treatment
options.D: Have higher conductivity needs early on

questions.explanation: The rationale for programmable (front-loaded) ultrafiltration is that patients can tolerate higher UF rates earlier rather than later in a treatment, so some machines allow the bulk of UF early or any custom UF profile. It is not about faster urea clearance later, avoiding hemolysis, or higher conductivity needs. Importantly, the chapter notes the clinical benefits of programmable UF have not been demonstrated by controlled studies, so the theoretical rationale is not backed by proven outcomes.

questions.question: Monitoring the ultraviolet light absorbance of spent dialysate leaving the dialyzer allows estimation of:

options.D: An online Kt/V, reflecting the change in blood urea concentration

questions.explanation: Monitoring the ultraviolet light absorbance of spent dialysate as it leaves the dialyzer tracks the concentration of small-molecular-weight substances over the session; the resulting curve reflects the change in blood urea concentration and can be used to calculate an online Kt/V. It does not measure dialysate temperature, membrane surface area, or priming volume. This gives a real-time, noninvasive estimate of delivered dialysis dose without repeated blood sampling, using urea as the marker whose fall the UV curve mirrors.

questions.question: Online conductivity-based clearance monitoring estimates dialyzer urea clearance by measuring how a deliberate change in dialysate sodium is attenuated between which two points?

options.A: Conductivity sensors at the dialysis solution inflow and dialysate outflow lines
options.B: Two sensors on the arterial blood line
options.C: The venous chamber and the drain
options.D: The prepump and postpump blood monitors

questions.explanation: Online conductivity-based clearance monitoring works because sodium clearance is similar to urea clearance. The machine briefly changes the concentrate-to-water proportioning to create a sodium 'pulse'; a conductivity sensor at the dialysis solution inflow line measures the perturbation, and a second sensor at the dialysate outflow line measures how much it was attenuated crossing the dialyzer. The relevant sensors are therefore on the dialysate inflow and outflow lines, not on the arterial blood line or between the venous chamber and drain. Combined with V and treatment time, this yields Kt/V at any point.

questions.question: The blood temperature control module improves hemodynamic stability by achieving:

options.B: An isothermic dialysis through controlled heat balance
options.C: Rapid cooling to below 35 degrees C

questions.explanation: Hemodialysis causes a heat gain that leads to vasodilation and a fall in blood pressure. By monitoring the temperature of incoming and exiting blood as well as the dialysis solution, the blood temperature control module controls heat balance to achieve an 'isothermic' dialysis, improving hemodynamic stability. It does not aim for maximum heat gain, rapid cooling below 35 degrees C, or higher sodium delivery. The same module can also be used to measure access recirculation or blood flow by the dilution principle.

questions.question: Modules that measure access recirculation during dialysis work on which principle?

options.B: Blood leak detection at the drip chamber
options.C: The dilution principle, using a perturbation applied to the outflow blood
options.D: The conductivity of the concentrate container

questions.explanation: Recirculation modules work on the dilution principle: the composition of blood leaving the dialyzer is quickly altered by injecting about 5 mL of saline, acutely changing the UF rate to hemoconcentrate, or cooling the returning blood, and a sensor on the inflow line detects the transmitted change in conductivity, hematocrit, or temperature. If recirculation exists, the outflow perturbation appears almost immediately at the inflow sensor, its magnitude reflecting the degree of recirculation. It is not based on P1 pressure, drip-chamber blood leak detection, or concentrate-container conductivity.

questions.question: Blood volume monitors use an ultrasonic or optical sensor on the inflow line to detect changes in hematocrit or plasma protein and may help to:

options.D: Anticipate and prevent hypotensive episodes by reducing UF

questions.explanation: Blood volume monitors use an ultrasonic or optical sensor on the inflow blood line to detect changes in hematocrit or plasma protein during fluid removal, which reflect plasma volume reduction. Their claimed value is anticipating and preventing a hypotensive episode by reducing UF when a steep hematocrit rise, or an approaching known 'crash crit,' is detected. They do not sterilize the dialyzer, adjust temperature, or measure concentrate conductivity. They can also flag covertly fluid-overloaded patients, who show minimal or no hematocrit rise despite fluid removal.

questions.question: Single blood pathway (single-needle) dialysis devices use a Y-shaped pathway, and their use is notably increasing in the context of:

options.A: Home dialysis, notably home nocturnal dialysis

questions.explanation: Single blood pathway (single-needle) devices use a Y-shaped single blood pathway instead of the usual two separate lines. They are used only rarely in the United States, but their use is increasing in the context of home dialysis, notably home nocturnal dialysis. They are not tied to acute ICU dialysis, pediatric plasmapheresis, or central delivery systems. The chapter treats their detailed description as beyond its scope precisely because they remain uncommon in routine US practice.

questions.question: The basic dialyzer shell is a box or tube with how many ports?
questions.explanation: The dialyzer shell is basically a box or tube with four ports: two communicate with the blood compartment and two with the dialysis solution compartment, with the semipermeable membrane separating the two. Two, three, or six ports are incorrect. This four-port arrangement is what lets blood and dialysate flow through separate compartments and interact only across the membrane, where solute movement between blood and dialysate occurs.

questions.question: In a hollow-fiber (capillary) dialyzer, the flow arrangement is that:

options.A: Blood flows around the outside while dialysate flows through the fibers
options.B: Both blood and dialysate flow through the same fibers
options.C: Blood flows through the fibers and dialysis solution flows around the outside
options.D: Blood and dialysate share a single central channel

questions.explanation: In a hollow-fiber (capillary) dialyzer, blood enters a header, passes through thousands of small capillary fibers bound in a bundle, and collects in a header at the other end, while the dialysis solution flows around the outside of the fibers. So blood is inside the fibers and dialysate outside, making option C correct and the reversed or shared-channel descriptions wrong. This large fiber surface area maximizes the membrane area available for solute exchange between the two compartments.

questions.question: Blood and dialysate flows are arranged to move in opposite directions in order to:

options.D: Maximize the concentration gradient between blood and dialysate throughout the dialyzer

questions.explanation: In both hollow-fiber and historic parallel-plate dialyzers, blood and dialysate flow in opposite (countercurrent) directions to maximize the concentration gradient between blood and dialysate in all parts of the dialyzer. Maintaining that gradient the whole length of the fiber keeps diffusion driving efficiently. It is not done to reduce priming volume, prevent air embolism, or lower transmembrane pressure. Countercurrent design is a fundamental reason dialyzers clear solutes effectively along their entire length.

questions.question: Which of the following is a synthetic polymer membrane used in the majority of clinically used dialyzers?
questions.explanation: The majority of clinically used dialyzers use synthetic polymer blends, including polysulfone, polyethersulfone, polyacrylonitrile (PAN), polyamide, and PMMA, so polysulfone is the correct example. Cuprophan, unsubstituted cellulose, and regenerated cellulose are cellulose-based, not synthetic. Synthetic membranes are more biocompatible than the historically used cellulose membranes, which, together with the perception that cellulose was low-flux, is why cellulose use has declined. Note that even different manufacturers' polysulfone membranes are not identical.

questions.question: Which statement about cellulose-based dialyzer membranes is correct?

options.A: Unmodified cellulose membranes such as Cuprophan are no longer in production
options.B: Cellulose membranes are more biocompatible than synthetic ones
options.D: Cellulose acetate can no longer be used clinically

questions.explanation: Unmodified cellulose membranes such as Cuprophan are no longer in production; their hydroxyl (OH) groups were largely responsible for poor biocompatibility, and they were historically perceived as low-flux, so their use has declined. They are not more biocompatible than synthetic membranes, are not always high-flux, and cellulose acetate can still be used clinically. Attempts to improve biocompatibility replaced the OH groups with acetate, yielding cellulose acetate, diacetate, and triacetate membranes that remain in clinical use.

questions.question: Coating a dialyzer membrane with vitamin E has been associated with which clinical benefit?

options.C: An improved antioxidant profile and, in some studies, reduced heparin needs and clotting
options.D: Elimination of the need for an air detector

questions.explanation: Coating a dialyzer membrane with an antioxidant such as vitamin E improves biocompatibility, and its clinical use has produced an improved antioxidant profile in patients' blood and, in some studies, reduced heparin requirements and reduced clotting. It is not associated with increased albumin loss, higher dialysate conductivity, or eliminating the need for an air detector. The benefit is thus an antioxidant and anticoagulation-sparing effect, one of several strategies to make membranes more biocompatible.

questions.question: The rationale for deliberately using membranes with high albumin permeability is to:

options.D: Remove protein-bound uremic toxins along with albumin

questions.explanation: Because some uremic toxins are tightly bound to albumin, one school of thought deliberately uses membranes with high albumin permeability so that protein-bound toxins are removed along with the albumin. Albumin is lost with such membranes, and their routine clinical use is not widespread. The goal is not to shorten dialysis time, increase sodium clearance, or prevent microbubbles. Related very-high-molecular-weight cutoff membranes are used to remove free light chains in light chain deposition disease while still restricting albumin passage.

questions.question: The ultrafiltration coefficient (KUF) of a dialyzer is defined as the:

options.A: Maximum urea clearance at infinite flows
options.B: Volume of plasma water filtered per hour for each mm Hg of transmembrane pressure
options.D: Total dialysate volume used per session

questions.explanation: The ultrafiltration coefficient (KUF), as defined in Chapter 3, is the volume of plasma water filtered in milliliters per hour for each mm Hg of transmembrane pressure. It is not a maximum urea clearance, a per-fiber surface area, or total session dialysate volume. KUF classifies membranes as low- or high-flux and determines how much TMP is needed for a given fluid removal. Manufacturer KUF values are usually in vitro and often 5-30 percent higher than the in vivo value.

questions.question: Broadly speaking, dialyzers with a KUF below 8 mL/h per mm Hg are low-flux, whereas those with KUF greater than which value are regarded as high-flux?
questions.explanation: Broadly, dialyzers with KUF below 8 mL/h per mm Hg are regarded as low-flux, while those with KUF greater than 20 mL/h per mm Hg are regarded as high-flux; those between 8 and 20 lie in an intermediate zone, with the higher part often behaving as high-flux due to beta-2-microglobulin passage. So 20 is the stated high-flux threshold, not 10, 15, or 30. High-flux membranes have large pores that pass larger molecules like beta-2-microglobulin (MW 11,800) and also have high water permeability, usually greater than 20 mL/h per mm Hg.

questions.question: For a dialyzer with a KUF of 2.0, roughly what transmembrane pressure is required to remove 1,000 mL/h of fluid?
questions.explanation: For a dialyzer with a KUF of 2.0, removing 1,000 mL/h requires 500 mm Hg of TMP, since fluid removal equals KUF times TMP. If KUF were 8.0, only 125 mm Hg would be needed, showing that high-KUF dialyzers need far less pressure and that small TMP errors then cause large errors in fluid removed. So 500 mm Hg is correct, not 125, 250, or 1,000. This is exactly why dialyzers with KUF above 6.0, and certainly above 8.0, should only be used with machines that provide accurate UF control.

questions.question: The mass transfer area coefficient (K0A) of a dialyzer represents the:

options.A: Maximum theoretical clearance for a solute at infinite blood and dialysate flow rates
options.D: Actual in vivo urea clearance at 200 mL/min

questions.explanation: The mass transfer area coefficient (K0A) is the maximum theoretical clearance of the dialyzer in mL/min for a given solute at infinite blood and dialysis solution flow rates. It is not a priming volume, a thickness-times-area product, or an actual in vivo clearance at 200 mL/min. For a given membrane, K0A is proportional to surface area, though gains taper off as area becomes very large. K0A for urea specifically measures dialyzer efficiency in clearing urea and similarly sized solutes.

questions.question: Dialyzers with a K0A for urea greater than 800 mL/min are generally used for:
questions.explanation: Dialyzers with K0A for urea greater than 800 mL/min are used for high-efficiency dialysis. Values below 500 are for low-efficiency dialysis or small patients, and 500-800 represent moderate-efficiency dialyzers suitable for routine therapy, so those options describe the lower brackets. High-efficiency is a relative term, and many modern routinely used dialyzers have in vitro K0A values of 1,200-1,600 mL/min. K0A thus ranks dialyzers by their urea-clearing capability.

questions.question: The dialyzer creatinine clearance is usually about what fraction of the urea clearance, providing little additional clinical information?
questions.explanation: The dialyzer creatinine clearance is usually about 80% of the urea clearance and provides no clinically useful additional information, because the clearances of the two molecules are almost always proportional regardless of membrane or dialyzer type. So 50%, 100%, and 120% are incorrect. Since creatinine (MW 113) tracks urea (MW 60) so predictably, reporting it adds little, which is why urea clearance and K0A remain the standard efficiency measures on specification sheets.

questions.question: The membrane surface area of most dialyzers suitable for adult patients ranges between:
questions.explanation: The membrane surface area of most dialyzers suitable for adult patients ranges between 0.8 and 2.5 m2, with smaller sizes available for pediatric patients. Ranges of 0.2-0.6, 3.0-5.0, or 5.0-8.0 m2 are outside the adult norm. Large-surface-area dialyzers usually have high urea clearances, since clearance depends largely on surface area times membrane permeability, though design and membrane thickness also matter. Surface area historically affected biocompatibility with unsubstituted cellulose but is less important with today's synthetic membranes.

questions.question: Ethylene oxide has lost popularity as a dialyzer sterilant primarily because of:

options.C: Rare but serious anaphylactic reactions in allergic patients and environmental concerns

questions.explanation: Among the four primary sterilization methods (electron-beam, gamma-irradiation, steam autoclaving, and ethylene oxide gas), ethylene oxide has lost popularity because of the rare but serious anaphylactic reactions during dialysis in occasional patients allergic to it, plus environmental concerns. It is not due to poor bacterial efficacy, damage to synthetic membranes, or excessive cost versus steam. This is why most modern dialyzers are sterilized by electron-beam, gamma, or steam rather than ethylene oxide.
